presentation.hostLeadIn: Rules, rules, rules. It's truly a historical tradition as old as time.
wrongAnswerQuips: Greece preferred philosophy to these draconian laws.
Rome had its own laws, thank you very much.
Persia followed later, looking to copy the homework.

questions.question: Which 13th-century Venetian merchant wrote 'The Travels', detailing his journey along the Silk Road to the court of Kublai Khan?
questions.explanation: Marco Polo's account of his travels introduced Europeans to the culture and technology of Central Asia and China.

presentation.questionStyled: Which 13th-century merchant decided to write 'The Travels' after visiting Kublai Khan?
presentation.explanationStyled: Marco Polo's little travelogue was the original influencer post, introducing Europe to the wonders of Asia.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Travel was much more impressive before you could just fly there in a lounge chair.
wrongAnswerQuips: Columbus was trying to find a shortcut elsewhere.
Vasco da Gama liked boats more than writing.
Vespucci was busy having continents named after him.

questions.question: In what year was the first version of the Magna Carta sealed by King John at Runnymede?
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 and established the principle that everyone, including the king, is subject to the law.

presentation.questionStyled: In what year did King John finally decide to put a seal on the Magna Carta?
presentation.explanationStyled: 1215. A delightful moment when the king was finally told he wasn't quite above the law.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Even kings have to sign the paperwork eventually, don't they?
wrongAnswerQuips: 1066 was for conquerors, not for compromise.
1492 was for sailors, not for lawyers.
1588 was for ships of war, not liberty.

questions.question: The Inca Empire, which flourished in the Andes Mountains, was centered in which modern-day country?
questions.explanation: Peru was the center of the Inca Empire, with Cusco serving as its capital before the Spanish conquest.

presentation.questionStyled: The Inca Empire climbed high in the Andes, but which modern nation was their home base?
presentation.explanationStyled: Peru was the center of it all, with Cusco as the magnificent capital before things took a turn for the worse.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Mountain living at its peak, quite literally.
wrongAnswerQuips: Mexico had the Aztecs, not the Incas.
Brazil was doing its own thing, mostly jungle.
Argentina was far too south for this empire.

questions.question: What is the name of the effect where the frequency of a wave changes for an observer moving relative to its source?
questions.explanation: The Doppler Effect is the change in frequency of a wave in relation to an observer who is moving relative to the wave source.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the name of that physics phenomenon where a sound wave's frequency shifts as objects move?
presentation.explanationStyled: It's the Doppler Effect. Fascinating how simple relative motion can make a siren sound so dreadfully annoying.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I trust you understand basic physics, or is that too much to ask?
wrongAnswerQuips: The Photoelectric Effect involves light and electrons.
Bernoulli is busy explaining why planes actually fly.
Coriolis is just the reason toilets swirl funny.

questions.question: Which artist holds the record for the most Grammy Award wins as of 2024?
questions.explanation: Beyoncé surpassed Georg Solti in 2023 to become the artist with the most Grammy wins in history.

presentation.questionStyled: As of 2024, which musical artist has hoarded the most Grammy Awards in history?
presentation.explanationStyled: Beyoncé. She managed to edge out the classical conductor Georg Solti, which is quite the accomplishment.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Music awards: the ultimate vanity project for the ultra-talented.
wrongAnswerQuips: Solti held it for years until he was ousted.
Quincy Jones is impressive, but not enough.
Stevie Wonder is talented, but he's no record-breaker.

questions.question: Who directed the 1975 thriller 'Jaws', which is often credited as the first summer blockbuster?
questions.explanation: Steven Spielberg directed 'Jaws', a film that broke box office records and changed the way movies are marketed.

presentation.questionStyled: Who directed the 1975 film 'Jaws', officially ruining beach days for everyone forever?
presentation.explanationStyled: Steven Spielberg. He turned a giant rubber shark into the very first summer blockbuster.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Cinema is all about manipulation, and this film did it exceptionally well.
wrongAnswerQuips: George Lucas preferred space to the ocean.
James Cameron likes his disasters to be wet.
Coppola prefers crime families to hungry fish.

questions.question: Which show is the longest-running American animated program and sitcom?
questions.explanation: The Simpsons debuted in 1989 and has aired over 700 episodes, making it the longest-running American sitcom.

presentation.questionStyled: Which animated sitcom has managed to stay on television for an eternity?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Simpsons. Since 1989, it has aired over 700 episodes of yellow-tinted chaos.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Staying power is a rare trait, even in the land of animation.
wrongAnswerQuips: South Park is rebellious, but it's not the oldest.
Family Guy is just trying to catch up.
Arthur is charming, but nowhere near as long.

questions.question: Which film won the Academy Award for Best Picture in 2020, becoming the first non-English language film to do so?
questions.explanation: Directed by Bong Joon-ho, the South Korean film 'Parasite' made history at the 92nd Academy Awards.

presentation.questionStyled: Which film caused a stir in 2020 by being the first non-English Best Picture winner?
presentation.explanationStyled: Parasite. Directed by Bong Joon-ho, it was quite the shattering of conventions at the Academy Awards.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Sometimes the Academy actually gets it right. Very occasionally.
wrongAnswerQuips: Roma was brilliant, but it came up short.
Minari was lovely, but didn't win the big prize.
Crouching Tiger didn't make the Best Picture list.

questions.question: What is the street address of the fictional detective Sherlock Holmes?
questions.explanation: Sir Arthur Conan Doyle's Sherlock Holmes lived at 221B Baker Street in London.

presentation.questionStyled: Where exactly does that fictional detective Sherlock Holmes reside?
presentation.explanationStyled: 221B Baker Street. A legendary address for a man who didn't even technically exist.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A question of residence for the most famous amateur sleuth.
wrongAnswerQuips: Downing Street is for politicians, not detectives.
Privet Drive is for wizards, clearly.
Evergreen Terrace is where the cartoon family lives.

questions.question: Which Renaissance artist sculpted the marble statue of David found in the Accademia Gallery in Florence?
questions.explanation: Michelangelo's 'David' was created between 1501 and 1504 and is a masterpiece of Renaissance sculpture.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Renaissance sculptor carved the iconic marble 'David' in Florence?
presentation.explanationStyled: Michelangelo. He managed to create a masterpiece from a single block of discarded marble.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Art is a test of patience, something most people clearly lack.
wrongAnswerQuips: Bernini was a Baroque fellow, a bit later.
Donatello was good, but not that good.
Canova belongs to a much later century.

questions.question: Which composer's 'Ninth Symphony' includes a choral finale based on Friedrich Schiller's poem 'Ode to Joy'?
questions.explanation: Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 was the first major symphony to include vocal soloists and a chorus.

presentation.questionStyled: Whose Ninth Symphony dared to include a choral finale based on Schiller's work?
presentation.explanationStyled: Ludwig van Beethoven. A bold choice, really, to include voices in a symphonic setting.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Music that defined an era, and perhaps scared a few traditionalists.
wrongAnswerQuips: Mozart was brilliant, but not quite this loud.
Bach died long before this was composed.
Brahms spent his time worried about Beethoven's ghost.

questions.question: Which art movement, led by Claude Monet, focused on capturing the effects of light and color in outdoor settings?
questions.explanation: Impressionism originated in France in the late 19th century, with Monet's 'Impression, Sunrise' giving the movement its name.

presentation.questionStyled: Which art movement led by Claude Monet obsessed over light and color?
presentation.explanationStyled: Impressionism. It wasn't about the details; it was about the fleeting impression of light.
presentation.hostLeadIn: You either love the blurry paintings or you have actual taste.
wrongAnswerQuips: Surrealism is for melting clocks and fever dreams.
Cubism is for when you hate perspective.
Expressionism is for when you want to feel anxious.

questions.question: Which of these instruments belongs to the woodwind family?
questions.explanation: The flute is a woodwind instrument, even though modern flutes are typically made of metal.

presentation.questionStyled: Which of these orchestral staples is technically a woodwind?
presentation.explanationStyled: The flute. Despite being made of metal, it remains firmly in the woodwind category.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let's see if you know your way around an orchestra.
wrongAnswerQuips: Trumpets belong to the brass, obviously.
Cello is for the strings department.
Trombone is just a large, noisy brass pipe.

questions.question: Which artist is most famous for his Pop Art paintings of Campbell's Soup Cans?
questions.explanation: Andy Warhol was a leading figure in the Pop Art movement and used everyday consumer items as subjects.

presentation.questionStyled: Who transformed simple Campbell's soup cans into iconic pieces of Pop Art?
presentation.explanationStyled: Andy Warhol. He made the mundane extraordinary just by repeating it enough times.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Art for the masses, even if it's just canned lunch.
wrongAnswerQuips: Pollock preferred splashing paint to painting soup.
Lichtenstein was too busy with comic strips.
Haring was busy with chalk in the subway.

questions.question: Which Spanish surrealist artist is known for the 1931 painting 'The Persistence of Memory'?
questions.explanation: Salvador Dalí's 'The Persistence of Memory' is one of the most recognizable works of Surrealism.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Spanish surrealist gave us the drooping, melting clocks in 'The Persistence of Memory'?
presentation.explanationStyled: Salvador Dalí. He was as obsessed with dreams as he was with his own mustache.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Surrealism: because reality is clearly too boring for some people.
wrongAnswerQuips: Picasso would have drawn it with more cubes.
Miró was abstract, but not quite that surreal.
Kahlo painted herself, not clocks.

questions.question: Which Italian composer wrote the operas 'La Bohème', 'Tosca', and 'Madama Butterfly'?
questions.explanation: Puccini is considered one of the greatest composers of the verismo style of Italian opera.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Italian master composed the emotional juggernauts 'La Bohème' and 'Tosca'?
presentation.explanationStyled: Giacomo Puccini. He defined the verismo style with a flair for the dramatic.
presentation.hostLeadIn: If you enjoy weeping at the opera, you have him to thank.
wrongAnswerQuips: Verdi was busy with other dramatic scores.
Rossini was far too fond of comedic timing.
Bellini was lovely, but not quite the same.

questions.question: In which Dutch city is the Van Gogh Museum located?
questions.explanation: The Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam houses the largest collection of artworks by Vincent van Gogh in the world.

presentation.questionStyled: In which Dutch city would you find the museum dedicated to Vincent van Gogh?
presentation.explanationStyled: Amsterdam. It houses a staggering collection of his work for the tourist masses to admire.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A museum dedicated to a man who didn't sell a painting in his life.
wrongAnswerQuips: Rotterdam is too busy with its port.
The Hague is for international courts.
Utrecht is nice, but not the place.

questions.question: In which city were the first modern Olympic Games held in 1896?
questions.explanation: The first modern Games were held in Athens, Greece, the birthplace of the ancient Olympics.

presentation.questionStyled: Athens played host to the first modern Olympic Games. But when?
presentation.explanationStyled: 1896. A return to the ancient tradition, albeit with slightly better facilities.
presentation.hostLeadIn: A historic gathering of people trying very hard to run fast.
wrongAnswerQuips: Paris has had its turn, but not the first.
London was busy with industrial things.
Rome waited much longer for its turn.

questions.question: Which NBA team holds the record for the most championship titles as of the 2023-24 season?
questions.explanation: The Boston Celtics won their 18th title in 2024, moving them ahead of the Los Angeles Lakers.

presentation.questionStyled: Which NBA team just reclaimed the top spot for the most championship titles?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Boston Celtics. They took home their 18th title in 2024, finally shutting down the Lakers fans.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Basketball supremacy is a constant battle of fragile egos.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Lakers have had their share, certainly.
Golden State is modern history, nothing more.
Chicago is all about the nineties.

questions.question: Which tennis tournament is the only Grand Slam played on grass courts?
questions.explanation: Wimbledon is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and has been played on grass since its inception.

presentation.questionStyled: Which Grand Slam tennis tournament is played on traditional grass courts?
presentation.explanationStyled: Wimbledon. Nothing says high-society sport like playing on mown grass in England.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Tennis, or as I call it, 'hitting things with racquets'.
wrongAnswerQuips: US Open is hard courts, very harsh.
French Open is for people who love red clay.
Australian Open prefers a nice hard court.

questions.question: Which color jacket is awarded to the winner of The Masters golf tournament?
questions.explanation: The Green Jacket is the signature prize for winning The Masters at Augusta National.

presentation.questionStyled: What specific color jacket is the Masters winner forced to wear?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Green Jacket. A rather garish trophy for a game of golf, but there you have it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Golf: a good walk ruined, now with fashion disasters.
wrongAnswerQuips: Red is for anger, not winning golf.
Blue is for when you're feeling down.
Yellow is for bees, not golf champions.

questions.question: What color jersey does the leader of the general classification wear in the Tour de France?
questions.explanation: The 'maillot jaune' or yellow jersey is worn by the overall leader of the race.

presentation.questionStyled: In the Tour de France, what color is the leader's jersey?
presentation.explanationStyled: Yellow, or the 'maillot jaune'. It ensures everyone knows exactly who is currently winning.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Cycling through France seems like a lot of sweating.
wrongAnswerQuips: Pink is for the Giro, keep up.
Green is for those who like to sprint.
White is for the kids, clearly.

questions.question: Which driver holds the record for the most Formula 1 World Drivers' Championships, tied with Michael Schumacher?
questions.explanation: Lewis Hamilton won his seventh title in 2020, equaling Schumacher's long-standing record.

presentation.questionStyled: Who shares the record for the most Formula 1 championships with Michael Schumacher?
presentation.explanationStyled: Lewis Hamilton. Seven titles. It’s an impressive amount of driving in circles.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Driving fast is easy. Being the best is slightly harder.
wrongAnswerQuips: Vettel tried, but he fell short.
Max is fast, but he has a ways to go.
Alonso is still going, but the numbers don't match.

questions.question: What is the name of the trophy awarded to the winner of the Super Bowl?
questions.explanation: The trophy is named after Vince Lombardi, the legendary coach of the Green Bay Packers.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the Vince Lombardi Trophy awarded for in American sports?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Super Bowl. Named after a coach who probably yelled a lot at his team.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Football. The only sport that stops for commercials.
wrongAnswerQuips: Heisman is for college kids, try again.
Commissioner's trophy is for baseball, keep up.
Larry O'Brien is for basketball, obviously.

questions.question: Who created the Linux kernel in 1991?
questions.explanation: Linus Torvalds created Linux as a free operating system kernel while a student at the University of Helsinki.

presentation.questionStyled: Who is the programmer behind the Linux kernel?
presentation.explanationStyled: Linus Torvalds. He built it as a student project and now runs the world's servers.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Coding: the art of typing until things work by accident.
wrongAnswerQuips: Gates was too busy with Windows, thank you.
Jobs wouldn't be caught dead with Linux.
Stallman is too busy worrying about free software.

questions.question: Which programming language, created by Guido van Rossum, is named after a British comedy troupe?
questions.explanation: Van Rossum named the Python language after 'Monty Python's Flying Circus'.

presentation.questionStyled: Which programming language owes its name to a British comedy troupe?
presentation.explanationStyled: Python. Named after 'Monty Python', presumably because coding is a joke to most people.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Python: for when you want to look like you're working.
wrongAnswerQuips: Java is just for coffee breaks.
Ruby is for people who like diamonds.
Swift is for people who like to move fast.

questions.question: The name 'Bluetooth' was inspired by a 10th-century king from which region?
questions.explanation: The technology was named after Harald 'Bluetooth' Gormsson, a Viking king of Denmark and Norway.

presentation.questionStyled: Bluetooth technology was named after a Viking king. Which region was he from?
presentation.explanationStyled: Scandinavia. Harald Bluetooth, the king who apparently really liked wireless connectivity.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The things we name after ancient royalty are truly bizarre.
wrongAnswerQuips: Britain was busy being invaded.
Germany was far too busy with local drama.
Greece was busy doing philosophy, not tech.

questions.question: What is the name of the test designed to determine if a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior equivalent to a human?
questions.explanation: Alan Turing proposed this test in 1950 as a way to evaluate machine intelligence.

presentation.questionStyled: What is the name of the test that determines if a machine is actually intelligent?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Turing Test. Alan Turing, really just asking if a computer can fool you into thinking it's human.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Machine intelligence: an oxymoron for the modern age.
wrongAnswerQuips: Voight-Kampff is for finding replicants, keep your sci-fi straight.
Lovelace was a pioneer, not a test.
Pascal was for math, not AI.

questions.question: Which of these is a volatile type of computer memory that loses its data when power is removed?
questions.explanation: Random Access Memory (RAM) is used for short-term data storage while the computer is running.

presentation.questionStyled: Which type of memory is volatile, losing its data when the power dies?
presentation.explanationStyled: RAM. It’s useful for quick access, but utterly unreliable if you don't save your work.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Computers are notoriously fragile, just like your short-term memory.
wrongAnswerQuips: ROM keeps its memory, unfortunately for some.
SSD is for storage, it sticks around.
HDD is old tech that keeps your files safe.
